data.title: Brazil's Vale confirms $12 billion investment in Carajas complex through 2030
data.description:
Brazilian miner Vale confirmed on Friday that it will invest 70 billion reais ($12.26 billion) in its Carajas complex in northern Brazil through 2030.

Brazil's presidential palace had initially announced the planned investment on Wednesday. Vale added in a statement that the expected investments toward the "New Carajas" plan are in line with its guidance.

Carajas, which is located in Brazil's Para state, is Vale's largest iron ore production complex. It produced 177.5 million metric tons last year, or more than half of the firm's total output of the steel ingredient in the period.

Vale said on Friday it expects iron ore production at Carajas to reach 200 million tons per year by 2030, with the boost coming from moves such as the expansion of its Serra Sul mine.

The miner also sees its copper production at Carajas growing by 32% to about 350,000 tons by 2030.

Vale will hold an event with Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva later on Friday to provide more details on the investment plan.

($1 = 5.7110 reais)

data.title: Udemy jumps on Q4 results beat
data.description:
** Shares of online learning platform firm Udemy rise ~31% to a nine-month high of $10.22

** On Thursday, company posted Q4 sales of $199.9 million, above analysts' average estimate of $194.7 million - data compiled by LSEG

** Posts Q4 adjusted profit of 10 cents/shr, also above the estimate of 7 cents/shr

** Forecasts 2025 revenue to be between $787 million and $803 million, compared with analysts' estimate of $798.7 million

** Expects Q1 revenue to be between $195 million and $199 million, compared with the estimate of $197.7 million

** Shares of Udemy fell ~41% in 2024

data.title: COMMENT-US recap:EUR/USD tops 1.05 as USD falls for fourth day
data.description:
The dollar index fell for a fourth day on Friday, sliding as tariff concerns eased while U.S growth worries emerged.

Treasury 10-year yields tumbled to 4.45%, reversing gains since the January payrolls a week ago, after a report that retail sales fell by most in nearly two years.

EUR/USD rose as high as 1.0514, fueled partly by optimism about Ukraine peace discussions. Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said he was prepared to talk to President Vladimir Putin once Ukraine had agreed on a common plan with U.S. President Donald Trump and European leaders. Britain's foreign minister David Lammy said that he and U.S. Vice President JD Vance agreed Zelenskiy must be part of any peace talks.

Separately, the European Commission said it would react "firmly and immediately" against tariff increases.

USD/CHF fell below its 55-day moving average to a new year-to-date low of 0.8969.

Large 1.05 expiries and a nearby upper Bollinger slowed EUR/USD's ascent before reaching its year-to-date high at 1.0532. Options see upward momentum possibly fading with risk reversals still favoring euro puts.

Dollar weakness sent GBP/USD to a new year-to-date high of 1.2631, testing its nearby upper Bollinger. Momentum is currently working in the pound’s favor. A slew of U.K. economic reports will be eyed next week including employment and CPI. British Prime Minister Keir Starmer has discussed visiting the United States on a call with Donald Trump.

USD/JPY was pressured by falling Treasury yields though downward momentum stalled before it reached the key 152 level where options are stationed. Volatilities and skews fell across tenors suggesting the pair, while remaining in a bearish trend, may not move far from its 200-DMA at 152.74. Resistance is seen at its 100-DMA at 153.35 and 21-DMA of 154.16.

Treasury yields were down 3 to 6 basis points. The 2s-10s curve was down less than 1 basis point at +22.0bp.

The S&P 500 rose 0.10% as yields eased.

WTI oil fell 0.70% on expectations that a Ukraine peace deal would unleash global supply.

Gold fell 1.56% in a round of profit-taking while copper was little changed.

Heading toward the close: EUR/USD +0.35%, USD/JPY -0.30%, GBP/USD +0.23%, AUD/USD +0.65%, =USD -0.36%, EUR/JPY +0.04%, GBP/JPY -0.06%, AUD/JPY +0.33%.

For more click on

data.title: Diamondback in talks to buy Double Eagle for more than $5 billion, source says
data.description:
Diamondback Energy is in talks to buy EnCap-backed Double Eagle, a big West Texas energy producer, for more than $5 billion, a person familiar with the matter said on Friday.

Last year, Reuters reported that Double Eagle was exploring a sale of its Permian Basin-based producer in a deal that could be worth more than $6.5 billion, including debt.

A deal could be announced in the coming days, if the talks don't fall apart, and it is also possible another suitor could emerge, the person said.

In September, Diamondback acquired Endeavor Energy Resources for $26 billion to create an oil and gas company valued at more than $50 billion.

Diamondback and Double Eagle did not immediately respond to Reuters' requests for comment.

The Wall Street Journal reported on the talks first on Friday.

data.title: Why Dividend Stocks Should Be Your Valentine This Year — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Teresa Rivas

Roses are red; violets are blue. Headlines are crazy, but dividends stay true.

Income-hungry investors, after all, tend to crave stability. That resource might seem scarce in recent weeks, given the rapid-fire changes out of Washington and fears that the U.S. could cede its role as an artificial-intelligence leader. But research shows that the U.S. remains one of the best places for steady dividend payouts, and the dividends still serve as an excellent proxy for profits.

This Valentine's Day, it's hard to name a more iconic duo than U.S. companies' dividend increases and profit growth.

"Real world experience supports" the corporate finance theory that dividends are strong tells about management's optimism regarding the sustainability of earnings power, writes DataTrek Research Co-founder Nicholas Colas. There are no shortage of examples of big management shake-ups following dividend cuts, which are symptomatic of broader problems. By contrast, a steadily rising dividend is a sign that things are going well at a company.

"The same signaling mechanism applies to broad market indices," he writes. "Ideally, you want to see dividend payouts rise over time, just as would be the case with a healthy and growing individual company."

Of course, skeptics might be disheartened by the market's rocky relationship with dividend-paying stocks' lately, driven by higher interest rates, meaning the SPDR S&P Dividend exchange-traded fund has badly lagged behind the S&P 500 in the past year. (Why risk the volatility of a stock when bonds offer juicy yields?) Plus investors have interpreted Chinese AI firm DeepSeek's emergence as a threat to dividend-paying tech stocks.

But investors can still believe in the power of dividend stocks. After all, the fixed income market has its problems too — over the past 10 years, long-term Treasuries have posted a negative return for the first time in 90 years. There also are plenty of havens among domestic dividend payers from uncertain government policies like tariffs.

Most importantly, large U.S. public companies have delivered much stronger dividend growth than their non-U.S. peers over the past five years, which went hand-in-hand with better performance according to DataTrek's look at the performance of popular domestic and foreign ETFs. The range runs from a compound annual dividend growth rate of about 5% for the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF that tracks its namesake index to nearly 14% for the tech-heavy Invesco QQQ Trust. Their returns have outperformed overseas peers, too.

Still, it's a nuanced metric. Colas highlights that the equal-weighted S&P 500 and MSCI Japan have better dividend growth — nearly 7% and 9%, respectively — than the S&P 500's 5%, but worse five-year performance.

That's because investors still prize dividend growth from "growthier" companies over mature ones.

"The issue is one of sustainability. A tech stock with 5% dividend growth is more likely to outperform a consumer packaged goods company with 7% payout growth," Colas says.

Of course the equal-weight S&P 500 vastly diminishes the impact of Big Tech, many of which pay dividends. Japan is a unique case too: MSCI Japan is the only ETF whose five-year compound annual return trails its dividend growth over the same period. That shows how skeptical investors are of that nation's long-term growth, Colas notes.

Ultimately, higher dividend growth from the U.S. is another arrow in the quiver of the bull case for domestic stocks, Colas writes.

"We are long-term bullish on U.S. stocks over rest of world equities because their record of increasing hard cash dividend payments while still dominating global tech-enabled goods/services and growing earnings is second to none."

In fact, that's why Colas isn't concerned that some international ETFs — those tracking the MSCI Europe and emerging market indexes are outperforming the S&P 500 so far this year. He says that doesn't indicate any longer-term shift away from U.S. dominance.

"The American economy has been resilient and U.S. companies of all stripes (not just Tech) do a better job of generating strong equity market returns than their foreign counterparts," he concludes. "We remain positive on U.S. stocks."

That seems like good advice, even as the newsflow seems anything but steady-eddy. Love may be hard, but good dividends don't have to be.

Write to Teresa Rivas at teresa.rivas@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: TSMC considering running Intel's US factories after Trump team request, Bloomberg reports
data.description: Contract manufacturer TSMC is considering running chipmaker Intel's U.S. factories after requests from U.S. President Donald Trump's team, Bloomberg News reported on Friday.

data.title: Economic Data Leave Equities Mixed Intraday
data.description:
US benchmark equity indexes were mixed intraday as markets parsed data showing a drop in retail sales and a rise in industrial production in January.

The Nasdaq Composite was up 0.3% at 20,000.3 after midday Friday, while the S&P 500 rose 0.1% to 6,119.8. The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 0.2% to 44,622.5. Among sectors, financials and energy paced the gainers, while consumer staples saw the biggest drop.

In economic news, retail sales in the US fell more than market expectations last month amid declines in most categories, including the motor vehicle component, data from the Census Bureau showed.

"A resilient labor market, strong household balance sheets, and lower interest rates than a year ago mean we still expect consumer spending to rise at a solid pace in 2025," Oxford Economics said. "The proliferation of tariff threats takes off some of the shine, but are grounds for us to cut, not slash the forecast."

US industrial production increased more than projected in January amid gains in aerospace equipment and utilities, Federal Reserve data showed.

"Despite the monthly vagaries of the data, factory conditions are trending in the right direction," Oxford Economics said in a separate note.

The US two-year yield was down 5.8 basis points at 4.25% intraday, while the 10-year rate dropped 5.1 basis points to 4.47%.

In company news, Airbnb shares surged nearly 15%, the best performer on the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq. Late Thursday, the vacation rental company logged a bigger-than-projected fourth-quarter profit as strong demand propelled revenue above Wall Street's estimates.

Wynn Resorts was the second-top gainer on the S&P 500 intraday Friday, up 11%. The company late Thursday delivered a fourth-quarter beat.

Applied Materials shares dropped 6.8% intraday Friday, the steepest decline on the Nasdaq and among the worst on the S&P 500. Late Thursday, the semiconductor equipment manufacturer issued a downbeat fiscal second-quarter revenue outlook at the midpoint amid the impact of US restrictions on exports to China.

Magna International's US-listed shares decreased 4.4% after the Canadian auto parts supplier provided a full-year revenue outlook that fell short of the Street's expectations amid likely weakness in vehicle production.

West Texas Intermediate crude oil was down 0.7% at $70.75 a barrel intraday.

Gold fell 1.7% to $2,896.10 per troy ounce, while silver was little changed at $32.73 per ounce.

data.title: Henkel Product Winners: Product of the Year(TM) 2025
data.description:
Dial®, Persil®, and Schwarzkopf® brands win U.S.A Product of the Year™ awards

NORTHAMPTON, MA / ACCESS Newswire / February 14, 2025 / Product of the Year™ (POY) USA is the world's largest consumer-voted award for product innovation. The purpose of the award is to guide consumers to the best products in their market and reward manufacturers for quality and innovation. Each year, POY accepts entries for consumer products that demonstrate advancement within their industries. The POY USA seal is backed by a survey of 40,000 people by Kantar, a global leader in product innovation research.

Congratulations to the Dial®, Persil®, and Schwarzkopf® brands for winning 2025 Product of the Year USA Awards!

Dial® Healthy Hydration Body Wash - Winner in the Body Wash Category

The new Dial® Healthy Hydration Body Wash offers a unique combination of deep cleansing and effective hydration. This innovative formula leaves skin feeling clean and soft after just one shower. Healthy Hydration Body Wash contains ingredients such as Shea Butter Extract, Coconut Milk, and Hyaluronic Acid. With 50% more moisturizing ingredients (compared to Dial® Spring Water Body Wash), Dial® Healthy Hydration supports the brand's commitment to helping consumers feel confident and connected, all while "Dialing Up" their clean with every wash. Additionally, Dial® is proud to be Dermatologist Tested, PH Balanced, Paraben-Free and approved as cruelty free under the Leaping Bunny program. With a fresh lineup of launches for both men and women, Dial® brand is ready to resonate deeply with consumers and redefine the way they connect.

"It is an honor to have Dial®Healthy Hydration endorsed by Product of the Year as a 2025 winner for the Body Wash category," said Allison Feldman, Vice President of Marketing at Henkel. "At Dial®, we're committed to delivering products that not only give an undeniable clean but are also empowering. We learned from consumer insights that our audience is looking for body wash that provides both a thorough clean and effective hydration. We're very proud of this new Healthy Hydration range that will make high-quality personal care products accessible to everyone. We are excited to utilize the widely recognized and credible Product of the Year seal within upcoming marketing and sales efforts to guide shoppers towards a choice that 40,000 real consumers have found reliable and innovative."

Persil® Activewear Clean - Winner in the Laundry Category

Specifically designed for athletic wear while also working on all fabrics, Persil® Activewear Clean fights body

oil and sweat and eliminates odors, all while helping maintain fabric shape and stretch. The product is a first-of-its-kind variant for the brand, and was launched along with reimagined branding and additional variants in 2024.

"At Persil, we recognize that our consumers place high value in their clothes and, with the growth of athletic wear and synthetic clothing in recent years, we saw an opportunity to fill a gap in the laundry aisle and offer a solution for clothing that many people wear, day to day," said Matt Kutnick, Marketing Director of Persil. "Persil Activewear Clean helps maintain the delicate shape and stretch of performance wear while helping keep whites and colors vibrant so consumers can look and feel their best, wash after wash. For us, it's about providing a quality clean that also cares for the clothes our consumers love."

Schwarzkopf® Keratin Root Permanent Color and Keratin Root Temporary Color & Thickening Spray - Winner in the Beauty Category

The Schwarzkopf® Keratin Root Permanent Color is a permanent color treatment that refreshes the root of the hair by providing 100% gray coverage in just 10 minutes, leaving hair looking strong and healthy. The Schwarzkopf® Keratin Root Permanent Color kit contains the brand's exclusive bond enforcing system and a salon inspired 3-sided brush for precise application.

Schwarzkopf® Keratin Root Temporary Color & Thickening Spray with Keratin and Vitamin E is a temporary root color spray that instantly delivers up to 100% gray coverage and is scalp friendly. The formula is non-irritating and ammonia-free, and the non-sticky hold formula is delivered evenly and finely. This formulation helps ensure precise color application without mixing or mess that dries quickly and will wash out with your next shampoo.

"It is an honor to have Schwarzkopf® Keratin Root Permanent Color and Keratin Root Temporary Color & Thickening Spray endorsed by 2025 Product of the Year winners," said Christopher McClement, Senior Vice President, Marketing at Henkel Consumer Brands USA. "Evolution has been constant as the Schwarzkopf® brand has grown over the last century and our new range of Keratin Root products are a result of continuous research and development in an effort to elevate the hair care products and formulas we provide to our consumers. We are proud that this distinction - and the widely-recognized Product of The Year red seal used in connection with our products - will now be a symbol to consumers of Schwarzkopf Keratin Root's credible, reliable, and innovative hair color products, which they are sure to love."

View additional multimedia and more ESG storytelling from Henkel on 3blmedia.com.

Contact Info:

Spokesperson: Henkel

Website: https://www.3blmedia.com/profiles/henkel

Email: info@3blmedia.com

SOURCE: Henkel

View the original press release on ACCESS Newswire

data.title: Ben & Jerry's Accuses Parent Unilever of Banning Critical Trump Posts
data.description:
Unilever's Ben & Jerry's unit accused the parent company of blocking messages on social activism, banning posts critical of President Donald Trump.

In a Thursday letter to Judge Kevin Castel of the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York, Ben & Jerry's alleged Peter ter Kulve, Unilever's president of ice cream, unilaterally prohibited criticism of Trump until further review.

The executive said plans this year to restructure the ice cream business allegedly created a "new dynamic" in an "unprecedented time," according to the letter.

Unilever didn't immediately respond to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

data.title: Sector Update: Consumer Stocks Decline in Afternoon Trading
data.description:
Consumer stocks were lower Friday afternoon, with the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP) shedding 0.6% and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY) easing 0.1%.

In corporate news, Airbnb shares jumped past 14% after it outlined a full-year core earnings margin outlook that analysts said was higher than expected. Late Thursday, the company logged a bigger-than-projected Q4 profit as strong demand propelled revenue above Wall Street's estimates.

TreeHouse Foods posted lower-than-expected Q4 results and a downbeat 2025 revenue outlook amid slowing growth trends for the food category. Its shares were still up 4%.

Lottery.com shares jumped 17% after the firm said it has signed a memorandum of understanding with PlusEVO to buy an advanced lottery management system and gaming platform to support its international expansion.

data.title: GoDaddy Shares Drop After 4Q Earnings Miss
data.description:
By Chris Wack

GoDaddy shares were down 14% to $182.91 after its fourth-quarter earnings missed analysts' expectations.

The website-and-logo company said it had quarterly earnings of $198.6 million, or $1.36 a share, compared with $1.11 billion, or $7.72 a share, in the same quarter a year earlier. Analysts polled by FactSet were looking for earnings of $216.2 million, or $1.44 a share.

Revenue for the quarter was $1.19 billion, compared with the prior year's $1.1 billion and the $1.18 billion analysts were expecting.

The Tempe, Ariz., company said it had total bookings of $1.22 billion during the quarter, up 8.8% year over year.

Income for the year was down because the prior year's fourth quarter was inclusive of a non-routine, non-cash income tax benefit related to the release of the majority of GoDaddy's valuation allowance, said the company.

At Dec. 31, total cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments were $1.1 billion, total debt was $3.9 billion and net debt was $2.8 billion.

For the first quarter, GoDaddy expects revenue of $1.175 billion to $1.195 billion, with analysts expecting revenue of $1.19 billion, For the year, the company expects revenue of $4.86 billion to $4.94 billion, while analysts are forecasting revenue of $4.91 billion.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com

data.title: Sector Update: Health Care Stocks Retreat Friday Afternoon
data.description:
Health care stocks declined Friday afternoon, with the NYSE Health Care Index falling 0.8% and the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV) down 0.5%.

The iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB) eased 0.1%.

In corporate news, Moderna reported a Q4 loss of $2.91 per share, worse than the $2.83 loss expected in a FactSet poll. Revenue was $966 million, better than the consensus estimate of $942.8 million. Its shares rose 2%.

OS Therapies shares jumped nearly 24% after it said Friday it signed agreements to begin commercial manufacturing of its lead asset OST/HER2 as it prepares for a potential Biologics License Application submission to the US Food and Drug Administration.

West Pharmaceutical Services shares popped 10%. The company has a solid position in the injectable-drug containment market with "sticky recurring revenue," and the stock price has dropped to an "attractive entry point" after underwhelming Q4 results and 2025 guidance, Deutsche Bank said Friday in a note. Deutsche upgraded the stock to buy from hold and lowered its price target to $250 from $275.

data.title: Rally in US stocks may be broadening out - DataTrek
data.description:
Key points:
  • S&P 500, Nasdaq higher, Dow dips
  • Energy leads S&P sector gainers; Cons staples is biggest loser
  • Euro STOXX 600 index falls ~0.2%
  • Dollar, crude off, gold down ~1.6%; bitcoin up <2%
  • U.S. 10-Year Treasury yield falls to ~4.47%

RALLY IN US STOCKS MAY BE BROADENING OUT - DATATREK

The rally in U.S. stocks may finally be broadening out, Nicholas Colas, co-founder of DataTrek Research, writes in a note Friday.

While the benchmark S&P 500 is up about 4% for the year to date, the seven technology-related megacap companies, or the Magnificient 7, account for just 0.2 percentage points of that increase, he says.

Meta Platforms , which is on track Friday for a 20th straight session of gains and up about 26% for the year to date, is the only big contributor in that group to the S&P 500's gains so far in 2025, he writes.

"The other six names have collectively been a drag on the S&P," he writes, referring to Nvidia , Apple , Microsoft , Alphabet , Amazon.com and Tesla . Colas adds that a broadening out of the market rally is a "common sign of a mid-cycle market."

For most of the last two years, Nvidia and many of the other megacap names have driven gains in the stock market in a rally fueled by optimism over artificial intelligence.

A surge in popularity in late January of Chinese AI model DeepSeek had poured some cold water on that optimism.

Here is a graphic showing the performance of the seven largest stocks versus the rest of the S&P 500:

(Caroline Valetkevitch)

data.title: Sector Update: Tech Stocks Rise Friday Afternoon
data.description:
Tech stocks rose Friday afternoon, with the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) up 0.3% and the SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD) adding 0.5%.

The Philadelphia Semiconductor index was little changed.

In corporate news, Applied Materials shares fell 6.8%, a day after it issued a downbeat fiscal Q2 revenue outlook at the midpoint amid the impact of US restrictions on exports to China.

Meta is planning major investments into AI-powered humanoid robots, Bloomberg reported. Meta shares were rising 1.3%.

Nvidia has reduced its holdings in Arm by around 44%, Nvidia said in a regulatory filing Friday. Nvidia also divested all of its stakes in medical imaging specialist Nano-X Imaging , robot maker Serve Robotics , and voice AI company SoundHound AI . Nvidia reported new holdings in Q4 2024, including almost 1.2 million shares in AI-focused company Nebius and about 1.7 million shares in Chinese autonomous driving tech firm WeRide . Arm shares dropped 4.6%, Serve Robotics shares sank 43%, SoundHound fell 30%, and Nano-X Imaging slid 11%. WeRide shares rose 75% and Nebius shares were up 6.5%.

data.title: RanMarine Files for 1.85M ADS IPO
data.description:
By Stephen Nakrosis

RanMarine, maker of autonomous surface vessels that clean waterways, filed for an initial public offering of 1.85 million American depositary shares on Friday.

The company previously filed for an IPO in July 2023, which it withdrew last month.

RanMarine said it expects its IPO to price between $4 and $5 per ADS. Each ADS will represent one share of the company's ordinary stock.

The company said its autonomous surface vessels clean the surfaces of waterways while also capturing real time data on water quality.

RanMarine had sales of 649,880 euros ($682,503) in the year ended Dec. 31, 2023, and a net loss of EUR5.3 million. In the prior year, the company had sales of EUR432,427 and a net loss of EUR3.2 million.

The company said it applied to list on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the symbol RAN.

Write to Stephen Nakrosis at stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1359 ET - Economists Michael Feroli and Abiel Reinhart at JPMorgan expect that nearly all the expiring provisions of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act will get extended, given Republican control of Congress. Trump is also likely to try to make good on campaign promises to end taxes on tips and overtime, the JPMorgan team writes. All told, the TCJA extension would add about $4 trillion to $5 trillion to the U.S. federal debt by 2035. Other tax cuts like removing tips, overtime and Social Security payments from taxation could add anywhere from $1 trillion to $6 trillion more in debt, they estimate. Even high new tariffs would be "likely not enough to offset the deterioration due to extending TCJA," they find. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrosman)

1252 ET - Republic Services doesn't expect its operations to get hit by the economic implications of Trump tariff threats, at least for now. While the waste management company is mindful of the macro environment, CEO Jon Vander Ark says on a call with analysts, what happens with tariffs and the impact on inflation doesn't keep him up at night, he says. "We've lived through a pandemic and high inflation and war at the shore of Europe and other elements," Vander Ark says. Regarding Trump's approach to green energy, Republic says it didn't include the Department of Energy's compressed natural gas tax credits in its guidance for 2025. Shares rise 2.75 to $230.50. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1143 ET - The U.S. aims to squeeze Iranian oil exports as part of its "maximum pressure" strategy on Tehran, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent says in a Friday interview with Fox Business. "We are committed to bringing the Iranians to going back to the 100,000 barrels-a-day of oil exports," he says. Iran is currently exporting 1.5 million-1.6 million barrels per day, with export revenue used to fund terrorist activities, according to Bessent. "The Chinese, perhaps the Indians, are buying the sanctioned Iranian oil and that is unacceptable." Bessent says cutting Iran's oil exports and intensifying economic pressure will push the regime into severe economic distress. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1033 ET - Starbucks CEO Brian Niccol feels the company's history of resilience will serve it well as it now contends with rapidly changing considerations for tariffs. "As environments change, regulations change, laws change, obviously our job is to figure out how to navigate successfully through all that change," Niccol said in an interview with Bloomberg TV. "I think we've demonstrated over the history of Starbucks, we're pretty resilient when it comes to dealing with change." The coffee chain operates across 88 countries and has a global supply chain behind it, which may help as various components in its products and other goods may soon be subject to higher duties. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1023 ET - The Bank of Canada's more aggressive interest-rate cutting cycle compared to the Federal Reserve should weigh on the Canadian dollar in the near term, Convera analysts say in a note. The currency should remain structurally weaker after the BOC's policy easing ends, with rates expected to fall more than the Fed, they say. Additionally, uncertainty around trade tariffs and a Canadian government "without a strong leader" means monetary policy alone may not suffice to boost consumption and long-term productivity. The Canadian currency could recover in the second half if Canada renegotiates the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement without straining key services and a Conservative pro-business agenda takes effect. It could also receive support if global growth improves, boosting commodity prices. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1005 ET - Two Canadian market strategists believe tariff exhaustion is settling in among traders, with President Trump's threats of hefty, broad tariffs failing to materialize. "Investors seem to be smelling a rat on this tariff file," says David Rosenberg, head of Rosenberg Research, citing the 25% tariff reprieve for Canada and Mexico, and Thursday's announcement about reciprocal tariffs that produced no immediate action. Karl Schamotta, chief market strategist at forex firm Corpay, says that rightly or wrongly, fears of a global trade war are easing, citing declining 10-year Treasury yields and buoyant stock markets. Schamotta adds, though, that uncertainty is likely to remain elevated because "observers, including ourselves, are lacking a clear understanding of Trump's motivations." (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

0921 ET - The wave of reciprocal tariffs proposed by President Trump is raising the ire of trading partners globally. On the green energy side, Brazilian ethanol producers are protesting the potential measures, arguing that they 'make no sense' when comparing U.S. and Brazilian ethanol. "The measure aims to put ethanol produced in Brazil and the United States on the same level, even though they have different environmental attributes and decarbonization potential, and therefore it makes no sense to talk about reciprocity," says UNICA, a Brazilian trade group representing the sugarcane and renewable energy industries. U.S. ethanol uses corn as its main feedstock, while Brazilian ethanol uses sugarcane. Ethanol is a major consumer of corn for U.S. producers. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0859 ET - U.S. credit spreads have remained resilient, despite increased volatility in Treasury yields due to uncertainty around U.S. interest rate cuts and tariffs, Barclays analysts say in a note. "Despite the preponderance of policy-related headlines, credit markets have remained calm and well anchored near recent tights," they say. The resilient performance has been driven by strong investor demand and healthy corporate balance sheets, Barclays says. "Persistent yield-driven demand and still-intact fundamentals continue to underpin credit market stability." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0858 ET - A resolution to the Russia-Ukraine war by U.S. President Trump could be a boon for Europe, likely resulting in a joint EU issuance fund for reconstruction and development, Nomura analysts say. While real money clients cannot invest in bonds allocated to defence spending, they can invest in bonds allocated to reconstruction, the analysts say in a note. "Therefore, we could see a significant increase in joint EU issuance in 2025, which we believe would be well-received by investors," the analysts say. Nomura would also expect the bulk of winning tenders for rebuilding Ukraine to be European companies. "This would be a boon for European construction and manufacturing sectors, both of which have been languishing since the 2022 gas crisis." (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0817 ET - The euro could rise as much as 5% against the dollar if a Ukraine ceasefire is agreed followed by the resumption of gas supplies, J.P. Morgan analysts say in a note. However, whether this is realized will depend on the details, they say. Negotiations will require tough decisions on territories and de-militarization zones, Ukraine's neutrality, security guarantees and enforcement mechanisms. The resumption of lower-cost gas supplies, if at all, will likely be a smaller proportion compared to before Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022. The euro is also at risk of U.S. trade tariffs, while the U.S. economy remains strong. "A ceasefire wouldn't mean European's structural problems will disappear." The euro earlier hit a two-and-a-half-week high of $1.0491, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0815 ET - Germany will do its part to ensure that NATO can "stand on two equally strong legs," German President Frank-Walter Steinmeier says in a speech at the Munich Security Conference. This follows U.S. President Trump's demands that the European members of NATO and Canada spend more on defense. Europe has "got the message," Steinmeier says, adding that he has urged U.S. Vice-President JD Vance to engage with Europe. "One thing is certain: the new American administration has a very different world view than we do," Steinmeier says. "One that takes no account of established rules, partnership and established trust. We cannot change that. We must accept that and we can deal with it." (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0738 ET - NATO members must commit to a "strong timetable" to meet a higher defense spending target when they meet in the Hague for the alliance's annual summit on June 24-25, NATO's Secretary General Mark Rutte tells reporters on the sidelines of the Munich Security Conference. NATO countries must avoid the mistake of 2014, when they agreed to increase spending to 2% of their gross domestic product, but didn't set a timetable, leaving significant increases on hold until Donald Trump became the U.S. President for the first time in 2017, Rutte says. Trump recently called on NATO allies to spend 5% of their GDP, a figure that none of the 32 alliance members currently meets, not even the U.S. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Debt Increase From Trump Tax Policies Seems Likely — Market Talk
data.description: Economists Michael Feroli and Abiel Reinhart at JPMorgan expect that nearly all the expiring provisions of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act will get extended, given Republican control of Congress. Trump is also likely to try to make good on campaign promises to end taxes on tips and overtime, the JPMorgan team writes. All told, the TCJA extension would add about $4 trillion to $5 trillion to the U.S. federal debt by 2035. Other tax cuts like removing tips, overtime and Social Security payments from taxation could add anywhere from $1 trillion to $6 trillion more in debt, they estimate. Even high new tariffs would be "likely not enough to offset the deterioration due to extending TCJA," they find. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrosman)

data.title: Cathie Wood stands by $1.5M Bitcoin prediction despite ETF outflows: Finance Redefined
data.description:
Crypto investor sentiment continues to be pressured by global trade tensions between the United States and China, which resulted in nearly $500 million worth of outflows within three days, reinforcing analyst expectations of a forthcoming market bottom.

Despite some gloomy investor sentiment, ARK Invest’s Cathie Wood remains optimistic about Bitcoin’s trajectory to over $1.5 million by 2030, driven by continuing institutional adoption of the world’s first cryptocurrency.

Bitcoin price may reach $1.5 million by 2030 — Cathie Wood

Bitcoin’s chances of reaching $1.5 million are improving as institutional investors increase their exposure to digital assets, according to ARK Invest CEO Cathie Wood.

Bitcoin ( has been trading under the key $100,000 level since Feb. 4, as investor sentiment has been pressured by global trade war concerns following import tariffs announced by the US and China.

Despite the temporary market slump, Bitcoin’s odds of surpassing $1.5 million a coin have increased, according to Wood. 

“Many people know us for our [Bitcoin] bull case, $1.5 million,” said Wood during a video published on Feb. 11, adding:

“We actually think the odds have gone up that our bull case will be the right number because of what is becoming the institutionalization of this new asset class.”

Bitcoin retail, ETF outflows mount to $494 million; analysts eye market bottom

Retail investors are increasingly liquidating their Bitcoin holdings amid increasing institutional outflows and global geopolitical tensions.

The number of Bitcoin ( addresses with a non-zero balance sank below 52.5 million, marking a five-month low last seen in September 2024, Glassnode data shows.

In comparison, the Bitcoin network boasted over 52.6 million such wallets on Jan. 20, when Bitcoin reached an all-time high of $109,000, Cointelegraph Markets Pro data shows.

However, most selling pressure stemmed from the US spot Bitcoin exchange-traded funds (ETFs).

The Bitcoin ETFs recorded more than $251 million of cumulative net outflows on Feb. 12, marking the third consecutive day of net negative outflows, amounting to $494 million, Farside Investors data shows.

Still, some analysts say the crypto market is setting up for a reversal, based on growing accumulation among large Bitcoin holders known as whales.

Binance co-founder clarifies token listing process amid TST controversy

A Binance co-founder addressed concerns over the exchange’s token-listing criteria following the rapid rise and fall of the Test (TST) token, which briefly reached a $500-million market capitalization.

Most retail cryptocurrency investors allocate capital through centralized exchanges (CEXs) like Binance and Coinbase, with CEX-listed tokens getting significant attention and high investor demand.

The most important criterion for a token listing is its return on investment (ROI), which is calculated by comparing its first-day average price to quarterly performance across other CEXs, Yi He, co-founder of Binance, told Colin Wu in an interview published on Feb. 10.

Binance’s second benchmark is the project’s ability to bring innovation and new users to the industry that may “evolve into dedicated blockchain users over time.”

The third and final criterion, involving “high-profile projects with significant market buzz and valuations,” examines a token’s market performance on other major exchanges.

Ethereum TVL approaches three-year high — Will ETH price follow?

Ether’s (ETH) price has declined by 21% since Jan. 31, struggling to stay above $2,800 over the past week. Investor sentiment has weakened amid this underperformance, exacerbated by a 12% decline in the total cryptocurrency market capitalization over the same period.

Bulls continue to place their hopes on Ethereum’s dominance in total value locked (TVL), especially after the metric climbed to its highest level since 2022. Still, increased deposits don’t necessarily indicate higher network activity or greater transaction fee generation. 

TVL measures the value of assets deposited in smart contracts across various applications, including liquid staking, lending protocols, decentralized exchanges, yield farming platforms, crosschain bridges, tokenized assets and privacy mixers. Ethereum’s TVL reached 21.8 million ETH on Feb. 11, marking its highest level since October 2022. According to DefiLlama data, this represents an 11% increase over the previous month.

Lido v3 debuts institutional staking upgrade as US awaits staked ETH ETF

Lido, the largest liquid staking protocol, has introduced Lido v3, an upgrade designed to offer greater flexibility and composability for institutional Ether (ETH) stakers.

The update features stVaults, modular smart contracts that allow institutions to tailor staking setups, ensuring compliance and operational control, according to an announcement shared with Cointelegraph.

Lido v3 is “a major lead forward for Ethereum staking,” according to Konstantin Lomashuk, a founding contributor at Lido protocol.

“A significant percentage of Lido’s TVL already comes from institutions, and demand is only growing,” Lomashuk told Cointelegraph, adding:

“Lido v3, with stVaults at its core, is built to meet this need — giving institutions more control, flexibility and direct access to tailored staking setups.”

DeFi market overview

According to data from Cointelegraph Markets Pro and TradingView, most of the 100 largest cryptocurrencies by market capitalization ended the week in the green.

The PancakeSwap (CAKE) token rose over 95% as the week’s biggest gainer, followed by the Kaspa (KAS) token, up 26% over the past week.

Thanks for reading our summary of this week’s most impactful DeFi developments. Join us next Friday for more stories, insights and education regarding this dynamically advancing space.

data.title: Update: Gold Falls Off Record Even as Dollar and Yields Drop After Report Shows U.S. Retail Sales Sagged Last Month
data.description:
(Updates prices.)

Gold edged down from a record early on Friday even as the dollar and treasury yields fell after U.S. retail sales dropped more than expected last month, showing a slowing economy even as inflation continues to run hot.

Gold for April delivery was last seen down US$44.90 to US$2,900.50 per ounce, falling off Thursday's record high.

The U.S. Census Bureau on Friday reported January retail sales fell by 0.9% from December, down from a prior month rise of 0.7% and under the FactSet consensus estimate for a drop of 0.5%.

The weak data follows on this week's release of consumer and wholesale price reports, which said inflation continues to run well above the Federal Reserve's 2% target, lowering hopes for further interest-rate cuts this year.

The dollar was sharply lower following the report, bullish for commodities priced in the currency. The ICE dollar index was last seen down 0.63 points to 106.69.

Treasury yields also fell, with the U.S. two-year note last seen paying 4.261%, down 6.0 basis points, while the yield on the 10-year note was down 5.9 points to 4.477%.

data.title: CIBC Previews Tuesday's CPI
data.description:
Over at CIBC, Andrew Grantham noted the GST holiday will continue to impact headline CPI data in January, as that marked the only full month the holiday will be in effect. As such CIBC expects to see some further weakness in food prices, and declines for children's clothing and toys. Grantham said the flat reading that CIBC forecasts for the headline unadjusted change in prices during January would likely have been a 0.4% increase were it not for the GST impact. Gasoline prices were up on the month and likely one of the main causes of inflationary pressure, he added.

Grantham noted the Bank of Canada's Trim and Median gauges of inflation, as well as the old CPI-X, exclude the impact of indirect taxation such as GST and as a result shouldn't be as volatile as the headline measure. Actually, he said, it is possible that if the GST reduction wasn't passed on fully to consumers, trim, median and CPI-X may be printing stronger monthly readings than they otherwise would have. CIBC forecasts a slight acceleration in both trim and median on a year-over-year basis, mainly due to base effects.

On forecast implications, Grantham noted all measures of inflation are within the BoC's 1-3% bound at the moment, with some a little below and some a bit above the midpoint. This means policymakers can, for now at least, focus more on tariff uncertainty and the downside risks to GDP growth rather than the monthly wiggles in inflation, he said.

data.title: Correction to Texas Horse Estate Article on Feb. 13 — WSJ
data.description: The wood used in the construction of the Ford family's home and kitchen island is cypress and burnt cypress. "What Comes After Retirement? For One Horse, a $2.2 Million Home in Texas" at 9 p.m. ET on Feb. 13 incorrectly spelled it as Cyprus.

data.title: January Industrial Production Rises More Than Expected Amid Aircraft, Utilities Boost
data.description:
US industrial production increased more than projected in January amid gains in aerospace equipment and utilities, data from the Federal Reserve showed Friday.

Industrial output rose 0.5% last month following an upwardly revised 1% increase in December. The consensus was for a smaller 0.3% increase in a survey compiled by Bloomberg. Annually, industrial production gained 2% in January.

Manufacturing output fell 0.1% last month after a 0.5% gain in December. The index for durable manufacturing remained unchanged in January, while the nondurable component fell 0.3%. Both grew 0.6% each in December, the data showed.

Within durables, aerospace and miscellaneous transportation equipment output jumped 6% last month, while motor vehicles and parts declined 5.2%.

Aircraft and parts production is "healing" from late last year's work stoppage at planemaker Boeing , Oxford Economics Lead US Economist Bernard Yaros said in remarks e-mailed to MT Newswires. "Despite the monthly vagaries of the data, factory conditions are trending in the right direction."

Utilities output jumped 7.2% in January as demand for heating got a boost from cold temperatures, the Fed said. Mining output fell 1.2% following a 2% gain in December.

Oxford Economics said it sees industrial production this year taking a hit from potentially "larger and swifter" tariff increases on major US trading partners.

On Thursday, President Donald Trump signed a memorandum ordering the development of a plan to impose "reciprocal tariffs" on other countries. Earlier this week, he signed proclamations raising tariffs on metal imports to 25%. The European Union vowed to retaliate. Mexico and Canada also reportedly condemned Trump's move.

Last week, Trump delayed plans for 25% general import tariffs on Canada, which ING recently said was the biggest supplier of aluminum to the US in 2023. China has set out a series of retaliatory tariffs against the US, while the Trump administration paused the announced general tariffs on Mexico.

data.title: Top Midday Decliners
data.description:
Bellevue Life Sciences Acquisition shares sank 44% in recent Friday trading, a day after the company said its shareholders approved its proposed business combination with OSR Holdings.

More than 191,200 shares traded intraday compared with a daily average of about 8,000.

Informatica's stock holds limited upside in the medium term after the software development company reported a "messy/confusing" Q4, missing expectations on most metrics except unlevered free cash flow, RBC Capital Markets said in a note.

Goldman Sachs downgraded Informatica to neutral from buy, with a $20 price target.

Shares of Informatica sank 27%, with intraday trading volume catapulting to over 17.5 million versus a daily average of about 1.6 million.

GoDaddy shares slumped after the company reported lower Q4 earnings late Thursday.

Shares retreated 14% as intraday trading volume surged to more than 2.7 million from a daily average of roughly 1.2 million.

data.title: Digital Ally Closes $15 Million Public Stock Offering -- Shares Tumble
data.description:
Digital Ally closed its public stock offering Friday, raising $15 million in gross proceeds.

The company offered 100 million common units or pre-funded units at $0.15 per unit and $0.149 per pre-funded unit. Each unit consists of one common share or pre-funded warrant, one series A warrant to buy one share at $0.1875 per share, and one series B warrant to buy one share at $0.30 exercise price.

The company's shares were down more than 15% in recent trading.

data.title: SEC Case Against Binance Put on Hold — Market Talk
data.description:
1353 ET - A federal judge in Washington D.C. has granted a joint motion to put on hold the Securities and Exchange Commission's case against cryptocurrency exchange Binance for 60 days. The order came after the SEC made the request in a joint motion filed earlier this week with the crypto exchange. It proposed a 60-day stay while a new task force, set up by President Trump's acting SEC Chairman Mark Uyeda, works on a regulatory framework for cryptocurrencies. The judge ordered the two parties to file a status report by April 14. (mengqi.sun@wsj.com; @_MengqiSun)

1337 ET - After the financial crisis, years of a zero interest-rate benchmark from the Federal Reserve didn't do as much as some economists had expected it would to stimulate financial activity and growth. That led some economists to question whether the Fed's ability to spur bank lending had weakened. Mark Spiegel of the San Francisco Fed finds in new research that this "bank-lending channel" of Fed policy action has come back, however. Studying data from after the Fed's first post-GFC rate hike in 2016, Spiegel finds that banks' lending activity is once again responsive to monetary-policy shocks. Large banks remain a bit less sensitive to Fed policy than small and medium banks, he finds. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1334 ET - The number of rigs drilling for oil in the U.S. edged up by one this week to 481, which was 16 fewer than a year ago, oil services company Baker Hughes reports, while natural gas rigs increased by one to 101, down 20 from a year before. In the Dallas Fed's 4Q energy survey published last month, two-thirds of exploration and production executives who anticipate increasing investment this year beyond their previous estimate said they expect to invest more in drilling and completion of wells. A majority of executives also expect permitting times for drilling wells on federal lands to decrease over the next four years. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1310 ET - Some of the most-followed real-time estimates of 1Q GDP growth move lower after the disappointing January retail-sales data--but forecasters are still calling for a moderate economic expansion in the Jan.-to-March quarter. The economics team at Goldman Sachs lowers its 1Q GDP growth forecast to a 2% annualized rate, from 2.3% previously. The GDPNow model hosted by the Atlanta Fed now sees the economy growing at a 2.3% clip this quarter, down from 2.9% in the previous estimate. The New York Fed's version of a GDP-tracker model now sees a 3% GDP growth rate in 1Q, a slight decline from 3.1% a week ago. The Commerce Department's advance estimate shows that last quarter, the economy grew at a 2.3% annualized rate. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1252 ET - Republic Services doesn't expect its operations to get hit by the economic implications of Trump tariff threats, at least for now. While the waste management company is mindful of the macro environment, CEO Jon Vander Ark says on a call with analysts, what happens with tariffs and the impact on inflation doesn't keep him up at night, he says. "We've lived through a pandemic and high inflation and war at the shore of Europe and other elements," Vander Ark says. Regarding Trump's approach to green energy, Republic says it didn't include the Department of Energy's compressed natural gas tax credits in its guidance for 2025. Shares rise 2.75 to $230.50. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1224 ET - Republic Services' pricing is coming down as its cost inflation drops, CEO Jon Vander Ark says on a call with analysts. The waste management company's retention rate remains very healthy as it gives customers a price point that fosters loyalty, Vander Ark says. But one area the company isn't pleased with is the municipal space. "That's one on the restricted side, and that's just an area of the business in general where customers on balance are not paying their fair share," he says. "We put an enormous amount of innovation, capital and labor into that equation, and we have many attractive customers and contracts, but not all of them meet our standard," the executive says. "You'll continue to see us optimize that portfolio over the next few years." Shares rise 2.7% to $230.61. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1201 ET - Republic Services is accelerating its M&A investments and expects to deploy at least $1 billion toward acquisitions in 2025, CEO Jon Vander Ark says on a call with analysts. For the past few years, the waste-management company has invested around $500 million in these deals, Vander Ark says. "The outsized number this year is predicated off a really strong start already, so we feel really good about the momentum the team has, and things that are either closed or nearly closed on that front." The company expects most of its M&A agreements to come from environmental services in the first half, and the back half of the year to be more heavily slanted toward recycling and waste, the executive adds. The company's guidance for 2025 includes the financial contribution from four acquisitions closed to-date. Shares rise 3.1%, to $231.45. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1156 ET - Republic Services expects to benefit from its new recycling center, located in Indiana, in the second half of 2025, CEO Jon Vander Ark says on a call with analysts. The waste management company has completed construction at the Indiana Polymer Center and equipment commission is underway, Vander Ark says. Overall for next year, the company is looking at incremental revenue of around $70 million, and incremental earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of $35 million across its portfolio of sustainability investments. The company also expects to spend another $75 million on Polymer Centers this year. Regarding its renewable natural gas projects, the company says it expects to have seven new projects in 2025. Shares rise 3.1%, to $231.34. (sabela.ojea@wsj.com; @sabelaojeaguix)

1119 ET - DraftKings kicked off the year well with revenue and Ebitda higher than expected in January, Truist analysts say in a research note. Month-to-date in February, the company has seen strong customer acquisitions, retention and engagement, they say. And its sportsbook hold, or share of money made after winning bets are paid out, was 11% in January and 13% in February, against a total of 10.5% for 2024, the analysts say. During the Super Bowl, DraftKings reached the top spot in the app store for sports, saw a record $436 million in bets placed and had a favorable bet mix that generated its best single day yet for gaming revenue from sports-betting, the analysts say. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1106 ET - With its 4Q report, DraftKings put a bow on a 2024 that saw it clocking huge gains across a variety of metrics, Truist analysts say in a research note. The company acquired 3.5 million new customers at record low acquisition costs and grew its online sports-betting and iGaming handle, or the total amount wagered by bettors, by 21% from the year before, the analysts say. The company's structural hold, or share of money made after paying out winning bets, rose 70 basis points and its promotions rate dropped by 200 basis points, the analysts say. And importantly, 2024 was the company's first year with positive free cash flow, they say. DraftKings jumps 13% to $52.34. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1038 ET - DraftKings raised its 2025 revenue guidance by $50 million at the midpoint, a lift largely attributed to investments in the company's live betting operations, Benchmark analyst Mike Hickey says in a research note. Part of that investment was its acquisition of Simplebet, which is expected to enhance its live-betting capabilities and product offerings, the analyst says. Simplebet's technology is expected to boost DraftKings's parlay handle mix and structural sportsbook hold percentage, or the cut of money it makes after paying out winning bets, the analyst says. DraftKings surges 14%.(dean.seal@wsj.com)

1033 ET - Starbucks CEO Brian Niccol feels the company's history of resilience will serve it well as it now contends with rapidly changing considerations for tariffs. "As environments change, regulations change, laws change, obviously our job is to figure out how to navigate successfully through all that change," Niccol said in an interview with Bloomberg TV. "I think we've demonstrated over the history of Starbucks, we're pretty resilient when it comes to dealing with change." The coffee chain operates across 88 countries and has a global supply chain behind it, which may help as various components in its products and other goods may soon be subject to higher duties. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1353 ET - A federal judge in Washington D.C. has granted a joint motion to put on hold the Securities and Exchange Commission's case against cryptocurrency exchange Binance for 60 days. The order came after the SEC made the request in a joint motion filed earlier this week with the crypto exchange. It proposed a 60-day stay while a new task force, set up by President Trump's acting SEC Chairman Mark Uyeda, works on a regulatory framework for cryptocurrencies. The judge ordered the two parties to file a status report by April 14. (mengqi.sun@wsj.com; @_MengqiSun)

1337 ET - After the financial crisis, years of a zero interest-rate benchmark from the Federal Reserve didn't do as much as some economists had expected it would to stimulate financial activity and growth. That led some economists to question whether the Fed's ability to spur bank lending had weakened. Mark Spiegel of the San Francisco Fed finds in new research that this "bank-lending channel" of Fed policy action has come back, however. Studying data from after the Fed's first post-GFC rate hike in 2016, Spiegel finds that banks' lending activity is once again responsive to monetary-policy shocks. Large banks remain a bit less sensitive to Fed policy than small and medium banks, he finds. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1310 ET - Some of the most-followed real-time estimates of 1Q GDP growth move lower after the disappointing January retail-sales data--but forecasters are still calling for a moderate economic expansion in the Jan.-to-March quarter. The economics team at Goldman Sachs lowers its 1Q GDP growth forecast to a 2% annualized rate, from 2.3% previously. The GDPNow model hosted by the Atlanta Fed now sees the economy growing at a 2.3% clip this quarter, down from 2.9% in the previous estimate. The New York Fed's version of a GDP-tracker model now sees a 3% GDP growth rate in 1Q, a slight decline from 3.1% a week ago. The Commerce Department's advance estimate shows that last quarter, the economy grew at a 2.3% annualized rate. (matt.grossman@wsj.com, @mattgrossman)

1201 ET - Euro-denominated corporate bonds are expected to move in small ranges in the coming months as falling sovereign yields offset wider spreads, LBBW credit analysts say in a note. Yields on euro corporate bonds are likely to decline in line with sovereign bond yields as the European Central Bank cuts interest rates. Meanwhile, euro credit spreads could widen modestly as they are already at narrow levels and vulnerable to poor eurozone economic performance, the analysts say. "The prospect of falling key interest rates suggests a downward trend in capital market yields, which should compensate for the negative effect of a slight widening of spreads," they say. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1117 ET - The first challenge of the German government that will emerge from the federal elections on Feb. 23 would be finding an additional 70 billion euros ($73.3 billion) if the country commits to spending 3% of its gross domestic product, German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius says. Germany's Special Fund to modernize the German armed forces, worth 100 billion euros, will run out in 2028, and any extra cash can't come from cuts to welfare, because that would divide the German society and boost the far-right and populist parties, Pistorius tells the Munich Security Conference. NATO's Secretary General, Mark Rutte, told a press conference Thursday that alliance members should get ready to spend more than 3% of GDP on defense. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

1058 ET - Investment-grade credit and high-yield credit are likely to provide insulation against market volatility due to rising uncertainty around U.S. tariffs and central banks' monetary policy, UBS analysts say in a note. Credit assets also provide portfolio diversification, the analysts say. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1028 ET - Some economists are taking a glass-half-full look at the disappointing January retail-sales figures from the Commerce Department. Michael Pearce of Oxford Economics argues that poor weather and a less favorable seasonal adjustment were some of the biggest factors behind the 0.9% month-over-month decline. "A resilient labor market, strong household balance sheets, and lower interest rates than a year ago mean we still expect consumer spending to rise at a solid pace in 2025," Pearce writes. Inflation-adjusted consumption could still grow at a 2.6% annualized rate in 1Q, Pearce projects. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1023 ET - The Bank of Canada's more aggressive interest-rate cutting cycle compared to the Federal Reserve should weigh on the Canadian dollar in the near term, Convera analysts say in a note. The currency should remain structurally weaker after the BOC's policy easing ends, with rates expected to fall more than the Fed, they say. Additionally, uncertainty around trade tariffs and a Canadian government "without a strong leader" means monetary policy alone may not suffice to boost consumption and long-term productivity. The Canadian currency could recover in the second half if Canada renegotiates the U.S.-Mexico-Canada Agreement without straining key services and a Conservative pro-business agenda takes effect. It could also receive support if global growth improves, boosting commodity prices. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1019 ET - The Swiss franc is likely to strengthen as the European Central Bank has more room to cut interest rates compared to the Swiss National Bank, ING analysts say in a note. The SNB could cut rates by a further 50 basis points while the ECB looks set to deliver 100bp of cuts, they say. ING expects EUR/CHF to fall to 0.90 in 12 months from 0.9435 currently. However, optimism over a Ukraine ceasefire deal presents a risk to this view given the franc's safe-haven credentials. "Should there be any progress here, questions may be asked whether EUR/CHF needs to be trading this low - given it was trading around 1.05 when Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022." (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1018 ET - The dollar is expected to reach its peak for the year in the first quarter, according to Bank of America's latest forex and rates sentiment survey.This view likely reflects increased concerns around a U.S. economic growth slowdown and doubts that U.S. exceptionalism will persist, BofA analysts say in the February survey. "More investors now simply lack conviction for the rest of the year." The dollar's valuation and narrower rate differentials in favor of other currencies are also seen as potential headwinds. Long positions that bet on a stronger dollar are still considered the most crowded trade. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

1015 ET - Weak economic growth in the eurozone could result in poor performance in risk assets and equities, analysts at Pimco say in a note. "There is reason to remain cautious given the challenging macroeconomic outlook and what we view as complacent valuations," they say. The euro is expected to depreciate against the dollar given the economic challenges and threats of U.S. tariffs, Pimco says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1011 ET - The current uncertain environment provides a favorable landscape for bonds, says Pimco's Nicola Mai in a note. "With current yield levels significantly higher than those before the pandemic, there are attractive opportunities for fixed income investors," the economist and sovereign credit analyst says. European duration is attractive, particularly in the 5- and 10-year part of the curve, he says in a note. Pimco tends to be underweight the long end of the curve due to increased sovereign issuance and reduced central bank support. "Having duration exposure in portfolios also offers significant upside in the event of more adverse global trade scenarios," he says. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Comex Silver Ends the Week 1.44% Higher at $32.801 — Data Talk
data.description:
Front Month Comex Silver for February delivery gained 46.60 cents per troy ounce, or 1.44% to $32.801 this week

  • Up for four consecutive weeks
  • Up $1.85 or 5.98% over the last four weeks
  • Largest four week net and percentage gain since the week ending Jan. 31, 2025
  • Longest winning streak since the week ending Oct. 4, 2024, when the market rose for four straight weeks
  • Up six of the past seven weeks
  • Today it is up 15.10 cents or 0.46%
  • Up two of the past three sessions
  • Today's settlement value is the third highest this year
  • Highest settlement value since Wednesday, Feb. 5, 2025
  • Off 5.83% from its 52-week high of $34.831 hit Tuesday, Oct. 22, 2024
  • Up 46.37% from its 52-week low of $22.41 hit Wednesday, Feb. 28, 2024
  • Rose 39.94% from 52 weeks ago
  • Off 0.26% from its 2025 settlement high of $32.888 hit Tuesday, Feb. 4, 2025
  • Up 10.73% from its 2025 settlement low of $29.622 hit Thursday, Jan. 2, 2025
  • Off 32.65% from its record high of $48.70 hit Thursday, Jan. 17, 1980
  • Month-to-date it is up 2.09%
  • Year-to-date it is up $3.861 or 13.34%

All prices are calculated based on the settlement price of the current front month contract.

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet
